JACKSON – Fossil lovers, rock collectors, and budding paleontologists, get ready to pack up your fossils and hit the road!

The Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries, and Parks’ Mississippi Museum of Natural Science proudly presents its 23rd Annual Fossil Road Show on Saturday, March 7, from 10 a.m. to 3 p.m.

This hands‑on, family‑friendly event invites visitors of all ages to bring their fossil finds for on‑the‑spot identification by the museum’s team of expert scientists. Whether you’ve unearthed a curious tooth, a mysterious bone, or a strange imprint in stone, the Fossil Road Show is your chance to discover its ancient story.

A Prehistoric Adventure for Everyone Which Includes

PASCAGOULA – The Pascagoula Police Department is asking for the public’s help in locating a missing woman who was last seen Monday afternoon.

Police said Kiva Barnes, 46, was last seen around 4 p.m. on March 2, 2026, walking near the Regency Woods Apartments in Pascagoula.

Barnes is described as 5 feet 6 inches tall and weighing about 150 pounds.

Police are asking anyone who may have seen Barnes or who has information about her whereabouts to contact the Pascagoula Police Department at 228-762-2211 or send a message through the department’s social media page.

No additional information was immediately available.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PASCAGOULA – The Pascagoula Police Department is asking for the public’s help in locating a missing woman who was last seen Monday afternoon.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Pascagoula police searching for missing woman last seen near Regency Woods Apartments - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
HATTIESBURG – A police pursuit Monday evening ended in a crash involving two vehicles at a Hattiesburg intersection, according to the Hattiesburg Police Department.

Officers said they attempted to conduct a traffic stop shortly after 5 p.m. March 2 after spotting a gray Nissan being driven erratically.

Authorities said the vehicle fled from officers, leading to a brief pursuit. The Nissan later ran a stop sign and collided with a black Toyota at the intersection of Columbia Street and North Street.

Police said both the driver and passenger of the gray Nissan were apprehended at the scene of the crash.

The female driver of the Toyota and her juvenile daughter were transported to a local hospital for treatment. The driver and passenger of the Nissan were also taken to a hospital.

Officials said all injuries reported in the crash were non-life-threatening.

Charges for the occupants of the gray Nissan are pending as the investigation continues.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: HATTIESBURG – A police pursuit Monday evening ended in a crash involving two vehicles at a Hattiesburg intersection, according to the Hattiesburg Police Department.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Police pursuit ends in crash at Columbia and North streets in Hattiesburg - Darkhorse Press

RIDGELAND – The Ridgeland Police Department is asking for the public’s help identifying an unknown man wanted for questioning in connection with a bank scam involving a local resident.

Police said the incident occurred in late January 2026. The man is described as wearing a dark-colored hoodie with “Golden State Warriors” embroidered on the front.

Investigators asked anyone who recognizes the man or has information that could help identify him to contact the Ridgeland Police Department Criminal Investigations Division at 601-856-2121.

Tips may also be reported to Central Mississippi Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477) or through the organization’s website at www.centralmscrimestoppers.com .

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
PRENTISS COUNTY – Two Booneville men have been charged following a burglary investigation in Prentiss County, according to the Prentiss County Sheriff’s Department.

Deputies said the investigation began Feb. 6, 2026, after authorities received a report that a shed on County Road 1219 had been broken into. Several items were reported stolen from the structure.

During the investigation, authorities identified two suspects on Feb. 14 as Dustin Blade Asher, 23, and Austin Tyler Adams, 30, both of Booneville.

Both men were charged with burglary of a commercial building.

Adams also faces additional charges of possession of a weapon by a felon and conspiracy, and is being charged as a habitual offender.

Bond for Asher was set at $25,000, while Adams’ bond was set at $105,000.

The case will be presented to the next Prentiss County Grand Jury by Investigator Aaron Stolz. Investigators Torie Jumper and Kolby Knight assisted with the case.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PRENTISS COUNTY – Two Booneville men have been charged following a burglary investigation in Prentiss County, according to the Prentiss County Sheriff’s Department.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Two Booneville men charged in Prentiss County shed burglary - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
RANKIN COUNTY – Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced the sentencing of Byron Gage Tatum, 26, and Chesney Lynn Stanley, 23, after the two pleaded guilty to one count each of grand larceny in a case involving an elderly victim.

Fitch’s office said Tatum and Stanley stole nearly $20,000 from the victim. The case was investigated and prosecuted by the Mississippi Attorney General’s Office.

“Preying on senior citizens, who need and deserve our extra care and protection, will not be tolerated,” Fitch said in a statement. “I am proud of the diligent work of our investigators and prosecutors who ensured that justice was served in this case.”

On Feb. 24,, Rankin County Circuit Court Judge Dewey Arthur sentenced both Tatum and Stanley to 10 years in the custody of the Mississippi Department of Corrections, with one year to serve. The remaining time was suspended, with five years of supervised release and four years of unsupervised probation.

The two were ordered to pay $18,570.40 in restitution and $500 to the Crime Victim Compensation Fund, according to the attorney general’s office.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: RANKIN COUNTY – Mississippi Attorney General Lynn Fitch announced the sentencing of Byron Gage Tatum, 26, and Chesney Lynn Stanley, 23, after the two pleaded guilty to one count each of grand larceny in a case involving an elderly victim.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Two sentenced in $18K theft from elderly victim in Rankin County - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
ADAMS COUNTY – Search efforts are continuing in Adams County for a 31-year-old man who was reported missing last month, according to the Adams County Sheriff’s Office.

Silver Alert issued for missing Covington County man

Cornelius Elijah Allen was originally reported missing out of Covington County on Feb. 25. Authorities said Allen was believed to be traveling in a 2017 black Volkswagen Tiguan with Mississippi license plate 910 CVB.

On Feb. 26, the vehicle was located abandoned near the intersection of D.A. Biglane Road and Canal Street in Natchez. The Natchez Police Department towed the vehicle from the scene.

Investigators said additional evidence connected to the case was discovered the following day. On Feb. 27, Allen’s car keys were found along the bank of the Mississippi River near the Natchez port area.

The Natchez Police Department is leading the investigation, with assistance from the Adams County Sheriff’s Office and other partner agencies.

Search and rescue teams launched operations in the Mississippi River on March 3, searching until approximately 4:30 p.m. The Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries and Parks has also assisted with the search alongside local law enforcement agencies.

Search operations resumed Wednesday at approximately 9 a.m., with teams continuing to search along the Mississippi River and nearby wooded areas.

Authorities are asking the public for assistance in reviewing any available security or camera footage from the day Allen’s vehicle was believed to be in the area. Investigators are specifically requesting residents and businesses check footage recorded Feb. 25 between 4:51 a.m. and 1:46 p.m.

Officials said cameras located near D.A. Biglane Road, Canal Street, the Natchez Port area and surrounding riverfront locations may have captured information that could help investigators establish a more accurate timeline of Allen’s movements.

Anyone who finds anything unusual or relevant on camera footage is asked to contact the Adams County Sheriff’s Office at 601-442-2752 or the Natchez Police Department at 601-445-5565. Those wishing to remain anonymous may contact Southwest Regional Crime Stoppers at 888-442-5001.

The Adams County Sheriff’s Office and the Natchez Police Department said they appreciate the continued support and cooperation from the community as search efforts remain ongoing.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: ADAMS COUNTY – Search efforts are continuing in Adams County for a 31-year-old man who was reported missing last month, according to the Adams County Sheriff’s Office.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: UPDATE: Missing man’s vehicle found abandoned near Natchez riverfront - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
FORREST COUNTY – A traffic stop attempt Tuesday evening in Forrest County turned into a vehicle pursuit that ended with two arrests, according to the Forrest County Sheriff’s Office.

Deputies tried to stop a vehicle around 6 p.m. March 3, 2026, after suspecting it was involved in criminal activity, the sheriff’s office said. The driver refused to stop and fled, prompting a pursuit.

With help from the Lamar County Sheriff’s Office and the Hattiesburg Police Department, authorities stopped the vehicle and took the occupants into custody without incident, the sheriff’s office said.

The driver, Shaquille Catrell Adams, was arrested and charged with felony eluding law enforcement, possession of a controlled substance and reckless driving, according to the sheriff’s office.

A passenger, Tearra Gray, was arrested and charged with disorderly conduct for failure to comply with law enforcement commands, the sheriff’s office said.

Both were transported to the Forrest County Jail.

Sheriff’s officials said Adams has multiple prior felony arrests and is currently on probation with the Mississippi Department of Corrections. MDOC has been notified, and a probation revocation may be pursued.

The sheriff’s office said the investigation remains ongoing and thanked assisting agencies for their help.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: FORREST COUNTY – A traffic stop attempt Tuesday evening in Forrest County turned into a vehicle pursuit that ended with two arrests, according to the Forrest County Sheriff’s Office.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Vehicle pursuit in Forrest County ends with two arrests - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
ADAMS COUNTY – One of Adams County’s Most Wanted individuals turned herself in to authorities Monday afternoon, according to the Adams County Sheriff’s Office.

Terrinika L. White, 33, of Natchez, surrendered at the Adams County Sheriff’s Office on March 2 at approximately 2:30 p.m.

White was taken into custody on an indictment and processed into the Adams County Jail. Authorities said she was later released after posting a $5,000 bond.

According to the sheriff’s office, White faces charges that include false pretense, simple assault on a law enforcement officer and felony fleeing.

White is scheduled to make her initial court appearance March 5 at 9 a.m. in Adams County Circuit Court.

Sheriff Travis Patten encouraged individuals with outstanding warrants to turn themselves in and address their legal matters through the court system.

The Adams County Sheriff’s Office said it remains committed to locating and apprehending individuals wanted for criminal charges in the community.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: ADAMS COUNTY – One of Adams County’s Most Wanted individuals turned herself in to authorities Monday afternoon, according to the Adams County Sheriff’s Office.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Adams County most wanted suspect turns herself in - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON COUNTY — Two men initially charged with aggravated assault in a late February shooting in Gulf Park Estates are now facing upgraded charges of attempted murder, authorities said.

According to the Jackson County Sheriff’s Department, Christopher Paul Landry, 46, and Glen Guy Barton, 32, were formally charged with attempted murder following developments in the investigation of a Feb. 26 shooting near North 19th Street and Pickering Forrest Drive.

Sheriff John Ledbetter said deputies responded around 9:30 p.m. to reports of a shooting and found 47-year-old Nathan Lee suffering from two gunshot wounds after an altercation with another party. Lee was transported to a local hospital and is listed in stable condition.

Barton was initially arrested without incident and charged with one count of aggravated assault with a deadly weapon. Landry was later located and arrested on Derry Lane in Ocean Springs after the sheriff’s department received a Crime Stoppers tip regarding his whereabouts. He was taken into custody on an outstanding warrant for aggravated assault with a deadly weapon.

As the investigation progressed, authorities upgraded the charges against both men to attempted murder. Barton also faces additional charges of possession of a weapon by a convicted felon and tampering with physical evidence. Officials said Barton was out on bond for a previous felony charge at the time of his arrest.

On March 4, Barton and Landry made their initial appearances before Mark Watts in Jackson County Court. Both men are being held at the Jackson County Sheriff’s Department Adult Detention Center.

The case remains under active investigation, and additional charges or arrests may be forthcoming.

Anyone with information is urged to contact the Jackson County Sheriff’s Office at 228-769-3063 or Crime Stoppers at 877-787-5898.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON COUNTY — Two men initially charged with aggravated assault in a late February shooting in Gulf Park Estates are now facing upgraded charges of attempted murder, authorities said.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Charges upgraded to attempted murder in Gulf Park Estates shooting - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
The Clinton Fire Department is asking for the public’s support to help restore a historic 1964 Mack fire engine that could become a community parade truck and piece of local memorabilia.

Officials said the antique fire truck, which has been properly stored and is still somewhat drivable, recently traveled between Station 1 and Station 4 across town. The vehicle has also been featured as part of Station 1’s Christmas decorations in the past.

While the truck is no longer suitable for firefighting, the department said it has the potential to serve as a parade vehicle and a reminder of Clinton’s firefighting history if restored.

The request for donations comes after the department recently placed a 1955 Chevy fire truck up for sale. Fire officials said the decision was not made lightly but was necessary due to space limitations and financial priorities.

According to the department, restoring the 1955 Chevy could have cost between $50,000 and $100,000. Officials said it would have been fiscally irresponsible to spend that amount on a vehicle that could not serve an operational role.

The department also noted that it faces other financial needs, including competitive firefighter salaries, updated equipment and the eventual purchase of new fire engines. New fire apparatus can cost between $750,000 and $2.5 million each, according to officials.

After hearing concerns from residents about the sale of the 1955 Chevy truck, the department said the community now has an opportunity to participate in preserving a different piece of Clinton’s fire service history by contributing to the restoration of the 1964 Mack.

The restoration project includes work on the truck’s fuel system, clutch and brake systems, as well as restoring the wooden hose bed and improving the vehicle’s exterior finish. Planned repairs include refurbishing the fuel tank, inspecting fuel lines, rebuilding brake components, replacing deteriorated wood with oak planks and restoring the truck’s appearance through paint touch-ups or polishing.

Fire officials said community donations would help cover the cost of these repairs and bring the historic engine closer to its original 1964 condition.

The Clinton Fire Department said it appreciates the support it receives from residents throughout the year and hopes the community will join in helping restore the historic vehicle.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: The Clinton Fire Department is asking for the public’s support to help restore a historic 1964 Mack fire engine that could become a community parade truck and piece of local memorabilia.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Clinton Fire Department seeks donations to restore historic 1964 Mack fire engine - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
CLINTON – The City of Clinton unveiled its second Mississippi Blues Trail marker Thursday, recognizing the legacy of the historic Grits & Gravy Recording Studio and its role in Mississippi’s musical history.

The studio, once located inside the former Hilltop Theatre, was founded in the mid-1960s by Bob McRee and brothers Cliff and Ed Thomas. During its years of operation, the studio attracted several notable artists, including Dorothy Moore, Junior Parker, Barbara Lynn, Freddy Fender and the GRAMMY-nominated duo Peggy Scott & Jo Jo Benson.

City officials said the studio helped leave a lasting mark on the state’s music scene.

After the recording studio era ended, Mississippi College continued the building’s musical tradition by using the space as the Choctaw Band Hall. The building remained a place for music until it was eventually demolished in 2013.

The unveiling ceremony was hosted in partnership with Visit Mississippi, the Mississippi Blues Commission and Mississippi College. Following the ceremony, Mississippi College provided refreshments for attendees, including grits and gravy, in tribute to the studio’s name and legacy.

Although the studio building no longer stands, officials said its history will now be preserved through the Mississippi Blues Trail marker.

With the addition of the marker, Clinton joins more than 200 locations across the state recognized on the Mississippi Blues Trail, a statewide initiative that honors the people, places and moments that helped shape Mississippi’s reputation as the birthplace of America’s music.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: CLINTON – The City of Clinton unveiled its second Mississippi Blues Trail marker Thursday, recognizing the legacy of the historic Grits & Gravy Recording Studio and its role in Mississippi’s musical history.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Clinton unveils Mississippi Blues Trail marker honoring historic Grits & Gravy Recording Studio - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON – A woman is dead after she was hit by a car while riding a bike in Jackson.

According to police, the crash happened in the 4800 block of Chastain Drive, vehicle vs bicycle. Two individuals were riding on a bicycle when they were struck by a vehicle.

There is no known description of the vehicle. The man who was also on the bicycle was transported by AMR to a local hospital to be treated for his injuries.

Jackson Coroner Jeramiah Howard identified the victim as 21-year-old Kaitlyn Mangum Gast, from Brandon.

Police were called at 11:11 this morning to the scene.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
FORREST COUNTY – The Forrest County Sheriff’s Office Evaluation Center has been certified as a Designated Mental Health Holding Facility, according to the sheriff’s office.

The facility, sometimes referred to as “The Annex,” is one of only two locations in Mississippi to receive the designation.

Officials said the certification allows the center to temporarily house individuals who are legally required to receive mental health treatment through involuntary commitment but are waiting for placement at a long-term treatment facility.

The centers serve as secure locations where individuals can stay while awaiting transportation to a permanent treatment center.

While individuals are waiting for placement, the facilities must provide basic care and services. According to the sheriff’s office, this includes a place to sleep, food, medical care and access to necessary medications. Medical staff are also responsible for evaluating the person’s condition to determine whether intensive hospital care is needed or if a less restrictive environment may be appropriate.

Sheriff Charlie Sims said the certification reflects the dedication of the center’s staff and their commitment to providing care to those in crisis.

“The certification of the Forrest County Sheriff’s Office Evaluation Center as a Designated Mental Health Holding Facility represents a major step forward for our county and for the state of Mississippi,” Sims said in a statement. “Being one of only two facilities in the state to achieve this designation reflects our commitment to doing this work the right way. I am extremely proud of the men and women who work at the Evaluation Center. Their dedication, professionalism and compassion demonstrate that they truly care about the citizens entrusted to us during some of the most vulnerable moments of their lives.”

Officials said staff at the Evaluation Center worked extensively to meet the requirements necessary to achieve the certification.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: FORREST COUNTY – The Forrest County Sheriff’s Office Evaluation Center has been certified as a Designated Mental Health Holding Facility, according to the sheriff’s office.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Forrest County Sheriff’s Office Evaluation Center certified as mental health holding facility - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON – The Jackson Police Department is asking for the public’s help identifying a man believed to be involved in a homicide that occurred last year at a local gas station.

According to police, the man in a surveillance photo is a suspect in the shooting death of 22-year-old Melvin Bass.

UPDATE: Teen girls arrested in connection with Jackson homicide on Cooper Road

The incident occurred Oct. 19, 2025, at the Valero gas station on Cooper Road, where Bass was shot and killed.

Investigators are urging anyone with information about the suspect or the case to contact the Jackson Police Department at 601-960-1278.

Tips can also be submitted anonymously through Crime Stoppers by calling 601-355-TIPS (8477). Officials said tipsters could receive a reward of up to $2,500 if the information leads to an arrest.

Crime Stoppers does not require personal information from those submitting tips, only details related to the crime.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON — The Jackson State University community is mourning the loss of Elayne Hayes-Anthony, Ph.D., a respected educator, communications leader, and former acting president of the university, who passed away this morning, March 5, 2026.

Hayes-Anthony dedicated decades of service to advancing education, journalism and public communication in Mississippi and beyond. Known for her unwavering commitment to student success and academic excellence, she mentored countless aspiring journalists and communications professionals while helping strengthen JSU’s legacy as a leading historically Black university.

“As we mourn the passing of Dr. Elayne Hayes-Anthony, we remember her career as she remained deeply committed to the success of our students and to the advancement of journalism and media education at Jackson State,” said Interim President Denise Jones-Gregory, Ph.D. “Her leadership in the classroom, within her department and across the institution helped shape generations of communicators and storytellers. On behalf of the Jackson State University family, we extend our sincere condolences to her family, former students and colleagues.”

A proud alumna of Jackson State, Hayes-Anthony earned both her bachelor’s degree in speech communication with an emphasis in broadcasting and a master’s degree in educational technology from the university. She later earned a doctorate in organizational communication and broadcast law from Southern Illinois University Carbondale.

Throughout her distinguished career, Hayes-Anthony held numerous leadership roles in higher education and public service. She served as professor and chair of the Department of Journalism and Media Studies at Jackson State University, prior to her death, and previously chaired the Department of Communications at Belhaven University for 17 years.

In 2023, Hayes-Anthony was appointed acting president of Jackson State University, guiding the institution during a pivotal period and continuing her long-standing commitment to the university’s mission of educating and empowering future leaders.

Before her work in higher education administration, Hayes-Anthony built a groundbreaking career in broadcast journalism. She made history as the first Black woman to serve as an anchor, producer and reporter at WJTV Channel 12 in Jackson, Mississippi, helping open doors for women and people of color in the news industry.

Her influence extended beyond the classroom and newsroom. Hayes-Anthony served as president of the Mississippi Association of Broadcasters, becoming the first Black woman and journalism educator to lead the organization. She also served as assistant superintendent of communications for Jackson Public Schools and held numerous civic and professional leadership roles throughout the state.

Hayes-Anthony was widely admired for her passion for teaching, her commitment to community engagement, and her tireless advocacy for journalism and ethical communication.

She is survived by her husband, Eddie Anthony; her son, Willard Anthony; and her grandson, Aiden Anthony. Funeral arrangements will be announced by the family at a later date.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — The Jackson State University community is mourning the loss of Elayne Hayes-Anthony, Ph.D., a respected educator, communications leader, and former acting president of the university, who passed away this morning, March 5, 2026.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: JSU announces the passing of former acting president Dr. Elayne Hayes-Anthony - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel

JACKSON — A man has been charged with two counts of murder in connection with a Sunday night shooting that left two people dead in Jackson.

Michael Knight appeared Tuesday in Municipal Court, where bond was set at $2 million for two murder charges. Authorities allege Knight is responsible for the shooting deaths of Antonio Arnold and Glen McWilliams.

Officers with the Jackson Police Department were dispatched around 10 p.m. Sunday to the 5300 block of Dogwood Trail near Oakridge Road in reference to a shooting.

Upon arrival, officers found two deceased individuals: a Black male lying in a ditch and a white male lying in the street nearby.

The victims were identified as 31-year-old Antonio Arnold and 63-year-old Glen McWilliams. Authorities said next of kin have been notified.

The department’s Robbery/Homicide Unit and Mobile Crime Unit responded to the scene, along with the Hinds County Coroner’s Office.

The investigation remains active.

Anyone with information about the case is asked to contact the Jackson Police Department at 601-960-1234 or Crime Stoppers at 601-355-TIPS (8477).
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON — A man has been charged with two counts of murder in connection with a Sunday night shooting that left two people dead in Jackson.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Man gets $2 million bond in Jackson double murder case - Darkhorse Press

TUPELO — A man was hospitalized with life-threatening injuries Wednesday night after a shooting at a Tupelo apartment complex, authorities said.

Officers with the Tupelo Police Department responded around 9:40 p.m. March 4 to Monarch Creek Apartments, located at 1321 Ida B. Wells St., in reference to a shooting.

When officers arrived, they found an adult male suffering from multiple gunshot wounds. The victim was transported to North Mississippi Medical Center for treatment.

Investigators also discovered multiple vehicles at the scene that had been struck by gunfire.

No arrests have been made, and the case remains under active investigation.

Anyone with information is asked to contact the Tupelo Police Department at 662-841-6491 or Crime Stoppers of Northeast Mississippi at 800-773-TIPS (8477).
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: TUPELO — A man was hospitalized with life-threatening injuries Wednesday night after a shooting at a Tupelo apartment complex, authorities said.

YAZOO COUNTY – Deputies responded Thursday morning to a collision involving a train and a vehicle on Highway 49, according to authorities.

At approximately 11:30 a.m. on March 5, deputies were dispatched to the area of Highway 49 near Coker Road after receiving reports of a train striking a silver vehicle.

When deputies arrived, they found that there were no injuries reported. Officials said the train had passengers onboard at the time of the incident, and there was one person inside the silver vehicle.

Authorities said the crash resulted in minimal damage, and everyone involved was unharmed.

The incident remains under investigation.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
PIKE COUNTY — A Pike County jury found Charles Pittman guilty of aggravated trafficking of cocaine following a trial in Pike County Circuit Court on Tuesday.

Circuit Judge Michael M. Taylor sentenced Pittman to 30 years in the custody of the Mississippi Department of Corrections. Under Mississippi’s aggravated trafficking statute, the sentence must be served day-for-day without eligibility for early release.

Prosecutors said evidence at trial showed Pittman and a co-defendant who previously pleaded guilty traveled to Texas to obtain cocaine and transport it back to Pike County for distribution.

The case stemmed from a joint investigation by the Mississippi Bureau of Narcotics, Pike County Sheriff’s Department, Mississippi Highway Patrol and the Mississippi Forensics Laboratory.

The case was prosecuted by Assistant District Attorney Sebastian Harrell.

District Attorney Adams praised the officers and jurors involved in the case.

“This conviction demonstrates the commitment of law enforcement and prosecutors across our district to seek justice and accountability as they aggressively combat drug trafficking,” Adams said in a statement. “Let this serve as a clear message to drug dealers: we will continue to work together, one case at a time, to stop drugs from being brought into and distributed within the 14th Circuit Court District.”

The Office of the District Attorney also thanked the jurors who served and the officers who participated in the investigation, citing their dedication to community safety.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: PIKE COUNTY — A Pike County jury found Charles Pittman guilty of aggravated trafficking of cocaine following a trial in Pike County Circuit Court on Tuesday.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Pike County man gets 30 years for aggravated cocaine trafficking - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
JACKSON – State Auditor Shad White announced Thursday that special agents with his office have arrested a former Laurel public works director accused of misusing city equipment and attempting to conceal evidence related to the investigation.

According to the Mississippi Office of the State Auditor, Luther Russell, the former Laurel Public Works director, is accused of using heavy equipment owned by the City of Laurel to perform work on his personal property.

Investigators allege Russell instructed employees to load the city’s track hoe onto a city-owned low-boy trailer and transport the equipment to his property, where it was allegedly used for personal work.

Authorities also said the roadway outside Russell’s home was damaged when the heavy equipment was transported to the property. After the county instructed that the roadway be repaired, Russell allegedly ordered employees to disable the GPS tracking device on a city-owned vehicle to conceal the vehicle’s movements while repairing the road.

“Thank you to the agents in my office who did the hard work in uncovering this scheme,” White said in a statement. “We will continue to work closely with prosecutors to get record results for taxpayers.”

Russell faces up to $8,000 in fines and up to 30 years in prison if convicted.

Officials noted that all persons arrested by the Mississippi Office of the State Auditor are presumed innocent until proven guilty in a court of law.

The State Auditor’s Office encourages the public to report suspected fraud by visiting the auditor’s website and clicking the reporting link or by calling 1-800-321-1275 during normal business hours.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON – State Auditor Shad White announced Thursday that special agents with his office have arrested a former Laurel public works director accused of misusing city equipment and attempting to conceal evidence related to the investigation.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: State Auditor’s Office arrests former Laurel public works director - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
MACEDONIA – Firefighters with the Macedonia Volunteer Fire Department responded Wednesday afternoon to a fire that destroyed a single-wide trailer on Otis Lee Road.

Crews were dispatched to the 100 block of Otis Lee Road at approximately 5:30 p.m. on March 4 after receiving a report of a trailer fire.

Upon arrival, firefighters found flames visible from multiple sides of the structure, with the fire rapidly spreading throughout the trailer, according to the department. Crews deployed hose lines from the first arriving engine and began an interior attack.

Firefighters later transitioned to a defensive attack after structural instability and collapse occurred inside the trailer.

Crews remained on scene for about two hours to bring the fire under control and extinguish hot spots. The trailer was unoccupied at the time of the fire, and no injuries were reported.

After speaking with the property owner, officials determined the fire began while leaves were being burned in the yard. The fire spread out of control and eventually ignited the trailer, the department said.

North Forrest Volunteer Fire Department and Dixie Electric Power Association also responded to assist.

The Macedonia Volunteer Fire Department reminded residents that outdoor burning can quickly become dangerous, especially during dry or windy conditions. Officials urged residents to keep burn piles a safe distance from structures and to have a water source or other means of controlling the fire available before starting an outdoor burn.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: MACEDONIA – Firefighters with the Macedonia Volunteer Fire Department responded Wednesday afternoon to a fire that destroyed a single-wide trailer on Otis Lee Road.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Trailer destroyed in fire on Otis Lee Road in Macedonia - Darkhorse Press

JACKSON – A dead tree at LeFleur’s Bluff State Park has been given new life as a towering Tyrannosaurus rex sculpture thanks to the work of an award-winning chainsaw artist.

Source: MS Museum of Natural Science The Mississippi Museum of Natural Science announced that artist Dayton Scoggins recently completed the wood carving at the LeFleur’s Bluff Playground, turning the tree into a detailed T-Rex sculpture.

Using a chainsaw and other carving tools, Scoggins transformed the tree into the dinosaur figure, creating a unique piece of outdoor artwork for visitors to enjoy at the playground.

Officials with the museum and park are encouraging visitors to stop by the playground to see the sculpture and take photos with the new attraction.

Visitors who take pictures with the T-Rex are invited to share them on social media using the hashtag #lefleursbluffplayground.

LeFleur’s Bluff State Park is home to the Mississippi Museum of Natural Science and is part of the Mississippi state parks system.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
STARKVILLE — Corporal Colby Huffman of the Starkville Police Department is making steady progress in his recovery following a serious motorcycle accident that left him critically injured, his family announced Friday.

Huffman, who was escorting a funeral detail on Feb. 21 when the crash occurred, was airlifted to the University of Mississippi Medical Center in Jackson for intensive care. His family reports that neurologists are pleased with his recovery and that a recent CT scan shows his head injuries are healing well.

This week, Huffman reached important milestones in his rehabilitation. On March 3, he underwent surgery to repair a broken femur. Two days later, he had a tracheostomy to replace his endotracheal tube, a step aimed at helping him gradually breathe on his own. His family said doctors expect him to achieve independent breathing within days.

Looking ahead, Huffman’s family plans to travel Monday to the Shepherd Center in Georgia, a leading facility for traumatic brain injury rehabilitation, where he will continue intensive therapy.

Huffman, a dedicated member of the Starkville Police Department, was injured in the line of duty while serving the community. Officials and supporters continue to encourage residents to donate or participate in events as part of ongoing efforts to support the officer’s recovery.

To support Huffman and his family during this time, the community has organized fundraising efforts, including a GoFundMe page and a raffle sponsored by local businesses. Proceeds will help cover travel, food, and other expenses related to his recovery. GoFundMe donations can be made online , and raffle tickets are available at Triple A Jewelry, Army Navy Pawn Shop, True Grit Trading Post, or by contacting organizers through the Starkville PD’s Facebook page.

“Every bit of support will help ease the burden on our family and allow us to focus on being there for Colby during this challenging time,” his family said in a statement. “We are deeply thankful for the outpouring of prayers, encouragement, and generosity from our community.”

Anyone with information about supporting Corporal Huffman can contact the Starkville Police Department at 662-323-4200 or visit the department’s social media pages for updates.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: STARKVILLE — Corporal Colby Huffman of the Starkville Police Department is making steady progress in his recovery following a serious motorcycle accident that left him critically injured, his family announced Friday.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Community rallies behind Starkville officer as he continues recovery from motorcycle crash - Darkhorse Press

MERIDIAN – A citywide law enforcement operation in Lauderdale County resulted in multiple arrests and the seizure of illegal drugs and a firearm, according to the Lauderdale County Sheriff’s Department.

The operation took place March 5 and involved local, state and federal agencies working alongside the East Mississippi Drug Task Force.

Authorities said officers confiscated more than 7 grams of crack cocaine, more than 6 grams of methamphetamine and a firearm during the operation.

Officials reported that the effort led to four felony arrests, two DUI arrests and the apprehension of three fugitives.

According to the sheriff’s department, felony charges filed during the operation include possession of crack cocaine with intent to distribute, possession of methamphetamine and felon in possession of a firearm.

The East Mississippi Drug Task Force and the Lauderdale County Sheriff’s Department said they will continue working to reduce criminal activity throughout Lauderdale County.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: MERIDIAN – A citywide law enforcement operation in Lauderdale County resulted in multiple arrests and the seizure of illegal drugs and a firearm, according to the Lauderdale County Sheriff’s Department.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
From documenting literally everything on social media to checking your texts during conversations, modern life is increasingly dominated by screens. The Global Day of Unplugging, observed from sundown March 6 to sundown March 7, invites people to step away from their devices for 24 hours and reconnect with the real world.

NationalToday.com says the annual event challenges participants to take a break from smartphones, computers and social media and spend the day relaxing, reflecting, spending time outdoors or engaging face-to-face with friends and family. Organizers say the goal is simple: remind people what life feels like when they are not constantly connected.

For so many of us, that break is long overdue.

The Cost of Constant Connection

Digital devices have woven themselves into nearly every aspect of daily life. Phones, tablets, televisions and computers keep people informed and entertained, but researchers say excessive screen time can carry real consequences.

One of the most immediate effects is disrupted sleep, according to BiologyInsights.com. Screens emit blue light that suppresses the body’s production of melatonin, the hormone responsible for regulating the sleep cycle. When people use devices late into the evening, the brain interprets the light as daytime, delaying sleep and reducing its quality.

Too much screen time can also encourage sedentary habits. Hours spent scrolling or streaming often replace physical activity, contributing to higher body mass index levels and increased body fat. Screen use is also commonly paired with mindless snacking, further increasing health risks.

The strain is not just physical. Prolonged focus on screens can lead to digital eye strain, including headaches, dry eyes and blurred vision. Poor posture while using devices often causes neck and shoulder pain.

What Your Phone is Doing to Your Brain

Experts also warn that constant digital stimulation can affect how people think and learn.

Many online platforms are designed around rapid feedback in the notifications, likes and short bursts of content, and these can condition the brain to expect instant gratification. Over time, this may reduce attention spans and make it harder to focus on slower-paced tasks like reading, studying or deep problem-solving, according to BiologyInsights.com.

Heavy screen use has also been linked to weaker executive functioning skills such as working memory, planning and self-control. When the brain relies on fast-paced digital input, it becomes less accustomed to sustained concentration and independent thinking.

Passive screen consumption such as scrolling social media or watching videos can also replace activities that build stronger neural pathways, including reading, creative play and conversation.

Social Media and Emotional Well-Being

The psychological effects may be even more concerning.

Social media often presents curated versions of other people’s lives, encouraging comparisons that can contribute to anxiety and depression. Studies have found teenagers who spend four or more hours daily on recreational screen time are significantly more likely to report symptoms of both.

Excessive screen use may also weaken real-world social skills. Face-to-face interaction teaches people to interpret body language, tone and facial expressions, which are key components of empathy and emotional intelligence. When those interactions are replaced by text messages and posts, those skills can suffer.

Ironically, technology designed to connect people can sometimes increase feelings of isolation. While digital communication offers constant contact, it often replaces the deeper bonds built through in-person interaction.

The 24-Hour Reset

This is where the Global Day of Unplugging comes in.

The annual observance encourages people to create a full day without screens, making for a small but meaningful reset in an increasingly digital world. Participants are urged to use the time to read, exercise, spend time outdoors, play games, cook meals or simply talk with loved ones without interruptions from notifications.

Even short breaks from technology can help people become more aware of their digital habits.

Experts recommend simple strategies to make unplugging easier, including creating screen-free zones in homes such as bedrooms and dining tables, establishing a nightly “tech curfew” before bedtime and setting limits on recreational device use.

Perhaps the most important step is learning healthy habits, and this is where you can change things for your kids.

When parents put their phones away during meals, conversations and family activities, they reinforce the idea that real-world connections matter more than constant digital engagement.

For participants in the Global Day of Unplugging, the challenge lasts just 24 hours. But many discover the experience offers a reminder of something increasingly rare in modern life: uninterrupted time with the people and moments that matter most.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: From documenting literally everything on social media to checking your texts during conversations, modern life is increasingly dominated by screens. The Global Day of Unplugging, observed from sundown March 6 to sundown March 7, invites people to step away from their devices for 24 hours and reconnect with the real world.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Global Day of Unplugging encourages you to put down your phone and reconnect - Darkhorse Press

HATTIESBURG – Pet owners in Hattiesburg and Petal can have their dogs spayed or neutered for a reduced cost throughout the month of March, thanks to a partnership with the ASPCA.

According to organizers, the program offers the service for $35 per dog. However, officials said appointments for female dogs are now fully booked, with remaining availability limited to male dogs.

Pet owners booking online may still see regular pricing listed, but the discounted rate will be applied during check-in on the day of the surgery.

Participants must provide proof of a current rabies vaccination for their dog. If the animal does not have one, a free rabies vaccine will be available at the time of the appointment. A free parvo vaccine will also be offered if needed, courtesy of Petco Love.

The program limits appointments to four dogs per client, and all dogs must be brought on a secure leash.

Officials said the discounted services are available only to residents with a Hattiesburg or Petal address.

Appointments can be scheduled online at sopineshealthypets.org. Organizers noted that spots are limited and additional fees may apply.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: HATTIESBURG – Pet owners in Hattiesburg and Petal can have their dogs spayed or neutered for a reduced cost throughout the month of March, thanks to a partnership with the ASPCA.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Low-cost dog spay and neuter program offered in Hattiesburg, Petal - Darkhorse Press

A male inmate was found dead in his cell Friday at the Clarke County Jail, and state authorities have been called in to investigate.

Clarke County Sheriff Anthony Chancelor said the inmate was discovered around noon on March 6. The man was found in his cell by jail staff.

Chancelor said there were no apparent signs of foul play, and the death appears to be an apparent suicide.

The Mississippi Bureau of Investigation has been requested to take over and lead the investigation, which is standard procedure in deaths involving inmates.

Authorities have not released the inmate’s name. The investigation remains ongoing.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: A male inmate was found dead in his cell Friday at the Clarke County Jail, and state authorities have been called in to investigate.

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Special to Darkhorse Press
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
CLINTON – The Mississippi Institutions of Higher Learning has approved two new Master of Science in Nursing tracks for the School of Nursing at Mississippi College: one with a concentration in nursing education and the other with a concentration in nursing administration.

These options are fully online and join the school’s robust undergraduate and graduate offerings.

“The Mississippi College School of Nursing is expanding in significant ways,” said MC Nursing Dean Kelly Harden. “Applicants for these tracks aim to advance their education and leadership skills in today’s challenging healthcare environment.”

In addition, the school has also developed three fully online Doctor of Nursing (DNP) tracks that are currently in Stage II of IHL’s approval process.

The DNP in Nursing Education would be the first in the state, and the DNP in Executive Leadership would join only one other. Once approved by the IHL Board of Trustees, recruitment can begin.

Harden said nurses seeking admission to these tracks aim to become highly skilled educators and leaders who will improve healthcare, shape policy, lead change, and meet the needs of diverse populations in Mississippi and throughout the U.S.

“The addition of these MSN and DNP options to our portfolio is evidence of our commitment to our mission: ‘to cultivate mission-minded practitioners prepared to navigate complex healthcare systems with integrity, evidence-informed curiosity, and a commitment to continuous learning,’” she said.

MC Provost and Executive Vice President Mike Highfield said he is grateful to the IHL Board for acknowledging completion of Stage I in the approval process for the DNP program at MC.

“This is a milestone that reflects the strong work of our School of Nursing faculty and leadership in demonstrating both the clear workforce need and MC’s capacity to deliver a high-quality DNP program,” Highfield said. “While important steps remain, we are encouraged by the progress and remain committed to developing a program that will prepare advanced nursing leaders who will translate research into practice, improve patient outcomes, and strengthen healthcare education and systems across Mississippi.”

Remaining steps in Stage II of IHL’s approval process for the proposed DNP program include qualifying for the initial accreditation, the submission of a self-study for the DNP program, and hosting a site visit by the IHL’s nursing education staff.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: CLINTON – The Mississippi Institutions of Higher Learning has approved two new Master of Science in Nursing tracks for the School of Nursing at Mississippi College: one with a concentration in nursing education and the other with a concentration in nursing administration.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mississippi College adds two new online nursing Master’s programs - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Therese Apel
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.content_html:
MISSISSIPPI — Communities across the state will celebrate the third annual Mississippi Musicians Month this March with concerts, lectures, educational programs and special performances honoring the artists and traditions that helped shape American music.

The month long celebration highlights Mississippi’s influence on music genres ranging from Delta blues and gospel to jazz, country, rock, pop and classical music. Events will take place in communities across the state, recognizing musicians past and present and encouraging public participation through performances and cultural programming.

Mississippi Musicians Month was officially established in 2024 when Gov. Tate Reeves signed legislation designating March as the annual celebration. Organizers say the initiative was created to recognize Mississippi’s historic role in the global music landscape and encourage communities statewide to celebrate their local musical heritage.

Mississippi is often referred to as the “Birthplace of America’s Music,” producing influential artists whose work helped shape modern music. Early pioneers such as Charley Patton, Son House, Robert Johnson and Jimmie Rodgers helped define early blues and country traditions.

The state later produced internationally recognized performers including Elvis Presley, B.B. King, Muddy Waters, Jimmy Buffett, Faith Hill and opera star Leontyne Price. Mississippi’s impact also extends to songwriters such as Glen Ballard and Craig Wiseman, whose work has produced numerous chart-topping hits.

Organizers say the celebration is intended to include musicians and performances of all kinds, from singer-songwriters performing in coffee shops to blues festivals in the Delta and symphonic concerts.

“Great and timeless music is one of Mississippi’s chief exports,” officials with Opera Mississippi said. “By honoring the past while celebrating today’s artists and inspiring future generations, Mississippi Musicians Month affirms the state’s lasting role in shaping the soundtrack of American life.”

Founded in 1945, Opera Mississippi is the ninth oldest continuously operating opera company in the United States and the state’s only professional opera company.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: MISSISSIPPI — Communities across the state will celebrate the third annual Mississippi Musicians Month this March with concerts, lectures, educational programs and special performances honoring the artists and traditions that helped shape American music.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mississippi Musicians Month celebrates state’s musical legacy - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.author: Morgan Howard

JACKSON – The Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries and Parks announced that the 2026 spring wild turkey season will open March 14 and run through May 3.

According to the department, the extension of the season follows state law, which allows the season to continue through the weekend when the closing date falls on a Friday.

Under Mississippi Code §49-7-31.2(c), when the open wild turkey season ends on a Friday, the season extends until 30 minutes after sunset on the following Sunday. Officials said the provision applies to this year’s season dates.

The extension does not increase or otherwise affect the annual bag limit for hunters.

Additional information about hunting seasons and bag limits can be found on the Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries and Parks website.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.summary: JACKSON – The Mississippi Department of Wildlife, Fisheries and Parks announced that the 2026 spring wild turkey season will open March 14 and run through May 3.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/2026-.title: Mississippi spring wild turkey season set to open March 14 - Darkhorse Press

https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.content_html:
MADISON- After more than three years at the Webster Animal Shelter, Maggie is still waiting for the right person to take her home.

Maggie, a 5-year-old dog with a big personality and lots of love to give, has been at the shelter since March 2022. Shelter staff say she deserves a forever home after patiently waiting far longer than most dogs.

Maggie’s favorite things include soft squeaky toys, which she happily plays with and may even share with someone she trusts.

When it comes to people, Maggie tends to bond most closely with women, although she has connected with some men as well. Shelter staff say she would do best in a home without young children and ideally as the only pet.

However, Maggie has shown she can get along with certain male dogs with slow, proper introductions. Staff say she has formed bonds with a few of the male dogs at the shelter.

Maggie is an active dog who loves spending time outdoors. Despite being a little overweight at the moment, she enjoys running and would make a great walking or jogging companion.

Because she likes to explore, Maggie will need a secure environment. Shelter staff recommend a sturdy wooden or iron fence, a tall chain-link fence, or plenty of safe outdoor space where she can stretch her legs.

Maggie has been spayed, is heartworm negative and is up to date on all vaccinations, including rabies.

Her adoption fee is $100, and the shelter accepts cash or check.

Please go visit and give this sweet girl a chance. She has been waiting far too long for a forever home and she is so deserving!

Anyone interested in adopting Maggie or learning more can call the Webster Animal Shelter at 601-605-4729 or visit the shelter in person.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.summary: MADISON- After more than three years at the Webster Animal Shelter, Maggie is still waiting for the right person to take her home.
https://darkhorsepressnow.com/news/top-s.title: DARKHORSE DOGS: Meet Maggie: Loyal, playful pup waiting years for a forever home - Darkhorse Press
